questions.question: Explain the differences between singly linked lists, doubly linked lists, and circular linked lists. Also state one practical application where circular linked lists are preferred over singly linked lists.
keyPoints: Singly: one-way traversal, nodes contain data and next pointer
Doubly: two-way traversal, nodes contain prev, data, and next pointers
Circular: last node points back to first, eliminating NULL checks
Practical use: round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, undo functionality in editors

questions.question: Write a function in C to insert a node at the beginning of a doubly linked list. Also mention the time complexity of this operation.
keyPoints: Function should handle empty list case (head NULL)
Create new node, set its next to current head, update previous of old head
Update head pointer to new node
Time complexity: O(1) - constant time insertion at beginning

questions.question: (a) Write complete C code to delete a node from the end of a singly linked list. (b) Explain the challenges in deleting the last node from a singly linked list and how doubly linked lists solve this problem. (c) What is the time and space complexity for deleting from end in both structures?
keyPoints: For singly: traverse to second-last node, set its next to NULL, free last node - requires two pointers/traversal
Doubly: directly access previous node via prev pointer, O(1) deletion at end
Singly deletion from end: O(n) time, O(1) space
Doubly deletion from end: O(1) time if tail pointer maintained, O(n) if only head - both O(1) space

questions.question: Design a menu-driven C program to perform the following operations on a circular singly linked list: (i) Insert at beginning (ii) Insert at end (iii) Delete a specific element (iv) Display the list. Include appropriate structure definitions, main function with switch-case, and function implementations with proper edge case handling.
keyPoints: Define struct with data and next pointer, initialize head to NULL and last pointer for efficiency
Insert at beginning: create node, handle empty list case by making node point to itself, else insert and update last->next
Insert at end: similar to beginning but traverse to last node, or use last pointer for O(1)
Delete: search node, handle single node case (make list empty), update pointers appropriately
